author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Hi Robert.

As others have said, you need a flywheel and probably a freewheel mechanism to be able to store up and smooth out the power you're generating through pedal power.  

For the grinder, you are going to need a much higher surface speed, which means a bigger pulley on the drive side and / or a smaller pulley on the grinder itself, you'll get better grinding and the grinding action will have less tendency to stop the wheel itself.

And then for sharpening drills, just using a jig like that will not work properly.  You'll get drills with a totally conical point and no relief.  They might go in if you force, but the "back side" of the cutting edges will rub, this will generate heat, squealing, and burn out your drill bits very rapidly.

author: @aussie_Logic
text:
Put a very large flywheel on you pedal power and store you energy in the flywheel
for later use or just to keep the momentum going on you devices you use

author: @davidp8157
text:
Never thought about it at the time,  we had a hand cranked grindstone (internal  flywheel)  that we used until I bought a light industrial electric  one,  early eighties,  I wore about 1/2 an inch off it over perhaps 15 years, my old man said it had the stone replaced when he was younger,  so it was definitely his father's,  probably" borrowed " off his father  when he went farming   end of WW1,so probably at youngest now  we'll over  hundred years old.
  Definitely ARMSTRONG brand....
I know as a kid,  it took  lot to keep it spinning

author: @deezy_da_M_E
text:
Some suggestions for the dynapod. 
1: add a flywheel to get a smoother rotation.
2: add a freewheel off a bicycle so rotation is not fixed to the pedals and the flywheel can do its thing.
Those 2 things should help with operating the dynapod for most if not all practical applications.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
As I've watched your dynapod series, I can't help but think of how many people "off-grid" could find endless uses for it!!  This is amazingly versatile!!  😊

And the more I think, "someone should REALLY design a commercial model, with adjustable gear ratios, various directional fittings and mounting points".  Along with an assortment of ready-made attachments to pick and choose from.  (A smoothie-maker for sure!! 😋)

Honestly, you'd think the exercise bike industry would have hit on this idea by now.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text:
I honestly don't know why you'd feel this is ridiculous - this is a great alternative to electric power for some mechanical tools/appliances when off-grid living.  Not to mention the obvious use as a generator - a fit person could probably generate about 200 watts per hour.  While that may not sound like a lot, if you already exercise then it means you're now harnessing all that energy.  

The beauty of this is... you aren't dependent on how sunny or windy it is to get a predictable amount of power.  One hour of cycling would power my portable 50L refrigerator for an entire day.  Or enough power for LED's to light 300 square feet of living space for nearly a week for 4 hours after sunset.  (I use 2-watt lighting panels I've built for lighting.  One panel can sufficiently light about 80 square feet, so 8 watts per hour x 4 hours = 32 watts per day.  Divide 200 watts by 32 = 6.25 days.)

While this is built out of anything Rob can find, imagine if this were specifically engineered with a wide variety of flywheel weights, gearing ratios, directional fittings and mounting points.  The possibilities for use are endless, and you get a great exercise workout!!

I'd encourage you to reconsider the practical applications of this.  I'd LOVE to build or buy one to power an off-grid washing machine!!  It would beat the hand-crank method hands-down (excuse the pun), especially if I need to clean larger items like blankets or comforters.
